request.question: Who is the director of Scared to Death, Irvin Kershner or William Malone?

title: Supernova (2000 film)
Never Say Never Again
The Empire Strikes Back
The Luck of Ginger Coffey (novel)
RoboCop 2
Scared to Death (1981 film)
Irvin Kershner
Stakeout on Dope Street
Paul Coates
William Malone (director)
sentences: Supernova is a 2000 Swiss-American science fiction horror film written by David C. Wilson, William Malone and Daniel Chuba and directed by Walter Hill, credited as "Thomas Lee."
"Thomas Lee" was chosen as a directorial pseudonym for release, as the name Alan Smithee had become too well known as a badge of a film being disowned by its makers.
It was originally developed in 1988 by director William Malone as "Dead Star," with paintings by H. R. Giger and a plot that had been called ""Hellraiser" in outer space."
Jack Sholder was hired for substantial uncredited reshoots, and Francis Ford Coppola was brought in for editing purposes.
Various sources suggest that little of Hill's work remains in the theatrical cut of the film.
The film shares several plot similarities with the film "Event Horizon," released in 1997, and "Alien Cargo," released in 1999.
The cast featured James Spader, Angela Bassett, Robert Forster, Lou Diamond Phillips, Peter Facinelli, Robin Tunney, and Wilson Cruz.
This film was shot by cinematographer Lloyd Ahern and scored by composers David C. Williams and Burkhard Dallwitz.

Never Say Never Again is a 1983 James Bond spy film starring Sean Connery, directed by Irvin Kershner, produced by Jack Schwartzman, and written by Lorenzo Semple Jr. with uncredited additional co-writers Dick Clement and Ian La Frenais, from a story by Kevin McClory, Jack Whittingham, and Ian Fleming.
It is the second adaptation of Fleming's "Thunderball", which was previously adapted as the 1965 film of the same name.

Unlike the majority of James Bond films, "Never Say Never Again" was not produced by Eon Productions, but by an independent production company, one of whose members was Kevin McClory, one of the original writers of the "Thunderball" story, who retained the filming rights of the novel following a legal dispute over them.
The Empire Strikes Back (also known as Star Wars: Episode V – The Empire Strikes Back) is a 1980 American epic space opera film directed by Irvin Kershner.
Leigh Brackett and Lawrence Kasdan wrote the screenplay, with George Lucas writing the film's story and serving as executive producer.

The second installment in the original "Star Wars" trilogy, it was produced by Gary Kurtz for Lucasfilm and stars Mark Hamill, Harrison Ford, Carrie Fisher, Billy Dee Williams, Anthony Daniels, David Prowse, Kenny Baker, Peter Mayhew, and Frank Oz.
The Luck of Ginger Coffey, a novel by Northern Irish-Canadian writer Brian Moore, was published in 1960, in the United States by "The Atlantic Monthly" and in the United Kingdom by Andre Deutsch.
In Canada, it received a Governor General's Award.
The book was made into a film, directed by Irvin Kershner, and released in 1964.
Robert Shaw starred in the title role.
RoboCop 2 is a 1990 American cyberpunk action film directed by Irvin Kershner and starring Peter Weller, Nancy Allen, Dan O'Herlihy, Belinda Bauer, Tom Noonan and Gabriel Damon.
Set in the near future in a dystopian metropolitan Detroit, Michigan, it is the sequel to the 1987 film, "RoboCop".

Scared to Death, also known as The Aberdeen Experiment and Scared to Death: Syngenor (DVD sleeve re-title), is a 1980 B horror/science fiction film directed by William Malone and starring John Stinson.
Irvin Kershner (born Isadore Kershner; April 29, 1923November 27, 2010) was an American actor and director of film and television.

He gained notice early in his career as a filmmaker for directing quirky, independent films early in his career, later moving on to films such "The Empire Strikes Back", the James Bond adaptation "Never Say Never Again", and "RoboCop 2".
Stakeout on Dope Street is a 1958 crime film directed by Irvin Kershner and written by Andrew J. Fenady, Irvin Kershner and Irwin Schwartz.
It follows three teenagers who inadvertently get themselves involved in a drug ring.
It was the directorial debut of Kershner.
The film stars Yale Wexler, Abby Dalton, Morris Miller, Allen Kramer and Jonathon Haze.
The film was released by Warner Bros. on May 3, 1958.
Paul V. Coates (March 10, 1921 – November 16, 1968) was an American print and television journalist.
He was known for his popular daily newspaper column and as the host of the syndicated tabloid-style television series "Confidential File", developed by Coates and Irvin Kershner (1923–2010).
William Malone (born 1953) is an American horror filmmaker who directed such films as the 1999 remake "House on Haunted Hill", "Scared to Death", "Creature", and "FeardotCom".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
1) Evidence found:
- William Malone: passage [1] — "William Malone (born 1953)" and "'directed ... \"Scared to Death\"'." (passage [1]). Passage [2] — "'is a 1980 B horror/science fiction film directed by William Malone'." (passage [2]).
- Irvin Kershner: passage [3] — "Irvin Kershner" described as "an American actor and director of film and television" (passage [3]). Passages [4–7] list films he directed (e.g., "'Stakeout on Dope Street' ... 1958") but none mention "Scared to Death" (passages [4–7]).
- Scared to Death (film): passage [2] labeled "Scared to Death (1981 film)" includes "'is a 1980 B horror/science fiction film'" and notes alternate titles ("The Aberdeen Experiment", "Scared to Death: Syngenor") (passage [2]).

2) Ambiguities:
- The film page label is "Scared to Death (1981 film)" while the lede text says "is a 1980 ... film" (passage [2]) — year/label inconsistency.
- No passage shows any "Scared to Death" variant credited to Irvin Kershner; only William Malone is named in the provided passages. If multiple films share the title, those variants are not present here.

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entity: "Scared to Death" (the film page shown as "Scared to Death (1981 film)" in passage [2]) and the "William Malone (director)" page (passage [1]).
- Missing fact needed to answer the question: the film's authoritative credited director as shown on the canonical "Scared to Death" page (the lede/infobox 'Directed by' field) and confirmation whether any other film titled "Scared to Death" exists that is credited to Irvin Kershner.

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized next hops):
- Open "Scared to Death (1981 film)" page — check the lede, infobox 'Directed by' field, release year, and "Credits/Production" or "Alternative titles" sections to confirm the director and resolve year discrepancy. Search keywords: Scared to Death 1980 1981 "Directed by".
- Open "William Malone (director)" page — check lede and filmography section for an entry "Scared to Death" (and year). Search keywords: William Malone Scared to Death filmography.
- Open "Irvin Kershner" page — check lede/filmography to verify no "Scared to Death" credit and ensure no similarly titled work under his credits. Search keywords: Irvin Kershner filmography Scared to Death.
- If still ambiguous, fetch "Scared to Death (disambiguation)" or search for other films titled "Scared to Death" and check their pages/credits.

Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved and their 'Directed by' credits confirmed.
hop2_docs: Scared to Death (1981 film) | Scared to Death, also known as The Aberdeen Experiment and Scared to Death: Syngenor (DVD sleeve re-title), is a 1980 B horror/science fiction film directed by William Malone and starring John Stinson.

You Scare Me to Death | You Scare Me to Death is a posthumous album credited to Marc Bolan of T. Rex.  Released in 1981 by record label Cherry Red, it is first LP of material released after his death in 1977.

Syngenor | Syngenor is a 1990 B horror/science fiction movie that was a sequel to the 1981 film "Scared to Death".  The film was directed by George Elanjian Jr and written by Michael Carmody and Brent V. Friedman.  It features David Gale and Kathryn Noble.

Scared to Death | Scared to Death is a 1947 thriller film directed by Christy Cabanne and starring Bela Lugosi.  The picture was filmed in Cinecolor.  The film is historically important as the only color film in which Bela Lugosi has a starring role.

Let's Scare Jessica to Death | Let's Scare Jessica to Death is a 1971 American psychological horror film, directed by John D. Hancock and starring Zohra Lampert, Barton Heyman, Gretchen Corbett, and Mariclare Costello.  The film depicts the nightmarish experiences of a psychologically fragile woman in an old farmhouse on a Connecticut island.

Peter Hyams | Peter Hyams (born July 26, 1943) is an American film director, screenwriter and cinematographer, known for directing "Capricorn One", the 1981 science fiction thriller "Outland", "2010" (the sequel to Stanley Kubrick's ""), the 1986 action/comedy "Running Scared", the comic book adaptation "Timecop", the action film "Sudden Death" (both starring Jean-Claude Van Damme), and the horror films "The Relic" and "End of Days".
Scared to Death (disambiguation) | Scared to Death is a 1947 horror film starring Béla Lugosi.
response.summary_2: William Malone is the director of Scared to Death. William Malone directed Scared to Death, a 1980 B horror/science fiction film also released as The Aberdeen Experiment (DVD sleeve re-title).
